title: Whakairo Awa: Carving the River's Story / Te Whakairo i te Kōrero o te Awa
subject: Arts
culturalContext: Students will explore the art of whakairo (Māori carving) through the story of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (The Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway to Ngāti Kahungunu that flows near our college. The stream's name references the mangakōtukutuku (blue duck/whio), a treasured native species, and its patterns and flow inform traditional carving designs that tell stories of connection, sustenance, and environmental guardianship.
learningObjectives: Identify and explain at least three key patterns (kowhaiwhai) used in Ngāti Kahungunu whakairo that represent elements of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.
Design and create a small-scale whakairo panel or tokotoko (walking stick) that visually narrates a personal or learned story connected to the awa.
Articulate, through a written or oral reflection, how their artwork embodies the concept of kaitiakitanga (guardianship) for local waterways.
Collaboratively contribute to a class waka huia (treasure box) digital gallery that showcases their artworks and the stories they tell.

activities.title: He Haerenga: Journey to the Awa
activities.description: The class will visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Students will sketch the flow of the water, the shapes of the banks, and any native flora/fauna observed (like the mangakōtukutuku/whio if sighted). They will listen to a local kaumātua or kaitiaki share the pūrākau (story) of the awa and its significance to Ngāti Kahungunu.

activities.title: He Whakawhiti Kōrero: Patterns of Place
activities.description: Back in the classroom, students will analyze traditional Ngāti Kahungunu carving patterns. They will work in pairs to match these patterns (e.g., mangōpare - hammerhead shark, representing strength; kape - symbols for growth) to the elements they observed and sketched at the awa, creating a visual 'story map'.

activities.title: He Whakaari: Designing our Kōrero
activities.description: Using their story maps for inspiration, students will design their own whakairo pattern or scene to be carved. This design must tell a story connected to the awa – this could be the historical pūrākau, a personal memory, or a statement about kaitiakitanga. Designs will be drafted on paper and then transferred to a piece of soapstone or a pre-prepared wood panel.

activities.title: Te Mahi Whakairo: The Art of Carving
activities.description: Using appropriate carving tools (v-tools, gouges, rifflers) and under strict safety supervision, students will carve their designs into their chosen medium. This is a practical, hands-on session focusing on technique, patience, and expressing narrative through form and line.

activities.title: He Kohikohinga: Curating our Waka Huia
activities.description: Students will apply a finish (paint, stain, oil) to their completed carvings. They will then write a short artist's statement explaining the story behind their work. Photographs of all artworks and statements will be compiled into a digital 'Waka Huia' (e.g., a Padlet or Google Site) to be shared with the school and local community.

resources: Local kaumātua or representative from Ngāti Kahungunu iwi to share pūrākau
Digital archive: 'Ngā Toi Māori' on Te Ara - The Encyclopedia of New Zealand
Carving tools (v-tools, gouges), safety equipment, and materials (soapstone blocks or pre-sanded wood panels)
The book 'Carving a Future: The Art of Whakairo' by Brian Flintoff

tasks: Submission of the completed carved artwork.
A written or recorded oral artist's statement (max. 250 words or 2 minutes) that explains the story behind the design and its connection to kaitiakitanga of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.
Peer feedback on another student's artwork within the digital Waka Huia gallery, focusing on the clarity of the story told.

nzcAlignment: The Arts - Visual Arts: Understanding the Arts in Context (UC), Developing Practical Knowledge (PK), Developing Ideas (DI), Communicating and Interpreting (CI)
Social Sciences: Understand how people remember and record the past in different ways.
Key Competencies: Thinking; Using language, symbols, and texts; Participating and contributing.
